input.content:
# The Desperado

> A piece that is already doomed grabs material, gives check, or creates a threat before it dies — selling itself as dearly as possible.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
**Prerequisites:** [Counting and exchanges](counting.md), [Intermezzo / Zwischenzug](intermezzo.md)

## The idea

A *desperado* is a piece that cannot be saved. Instead of letting it sit and be captured for nothing, you make it earn its keep on the way out: it captures an enemy unit, delivers a check, or sets up a fork or pin. The point is simple bookkeeping — if your piece is lost anyway, every pawn or piece it takes first is pure profit.

Two flavors dominate. First, the **loose-piece desperado**: one of your pieces is trapped or will be lost next move, so it gobbles something before the axe falls. Second, the **mutual-capture desperado**: both sides have a hanging piece in the same area, and whoever grabs first wins the tempo race. Recognizing "this piece is dead, so it costs me nothing to use it" is the mental switch.

## Why it matters

Most players, on noticing a piece is trapped, mentally write it off and play elsewhere — surrendering it passively. The desperado mindset converts a loss into a *trade* or even a net gain. In sharp middlegames where two captures hang simultaneously, the desperado is often the difference between winning a pawn and losing one. It also shows up constantly in endings, where a single extra pawn snatched by a dying piece decides the result. Train this and you stop "giving away" doomed pieces for free.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . b . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 . . R . r . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Both rooks are mutually attacked along the back rank and files — a classic mutual-capture race.*
**FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/2b5/8/6PP/2R1r2K w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/6pp/8/8/2b5/8/6PP/2R1r2K_w_-_-_0_1)

White's Rc1 is attacked by the bishop on c4; Black's Re1 is attacked by the king on h1. Instead of meekly defending or recapturing, White plays the desperado **1.Rxe1!** — the doomed rook (it was attacked by the bishop) dies *after* eliminating Black's rook. Now material is even and Black has only a bishop versus rook is not in play here — both rooks are gone and the game is balanced, whereas 1.Bxe1?? was not Black's move and passively losing the rook would have left White down. The race rewards the side who captures first.

```
8 r . b q k . . r
7 p p p p . p p p
6 . . n . . n . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . B . P . . .
3 . . . . . N . .
2 P P P P . P P P
1 R N B Q K . . R
  a b c d e f g h
```
*A doomed knight on f3 sells itself: the e-pawn is already pinned-style attacked.*
**FEN:** r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/8/2B1P3/5N2/PPPP1PPP/RNBQK2R b kq - 0 1
Side to move: **Black**
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/8/2B1P3/5N2/PPPP1PPP/RNBQK2R_b_kq_-_0_1)

This Italian-style position is balanced; the diagram above is for orientation. The desperado theme arises when a knight that will be captured first snatches the e4-pawn: a unit destined to be traded should remove material on its way off the board rather than retreat for free.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Loose-piece desperado:** your trapped piece captures the most valuable thing it can reach before being taken.
- **Mutual-capture race:** two pieces hang at once; capture *first* with the one that nets more or gains tempo.
- **Check-first desperado:** the dying piece gives check (or a discovered check) to win a tempo, then is recaptured under better terms.
- **Promotion desperado:** a passed pawn about to be stopped queens or captures a defender first.
- **Related motifs:** [Zwischenzug](intermezzo.md) (insert a move before recapturing), [Removing the defender](removing-the-defender.md), and clearance sacrifices.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Is one of my pieces lost or trapped] -->|No| B[Look elsewhere]
  A -->|Yes| C[Can it capture before dying]
  C -->|Yes| D[Grab the most valuable target]
  C -->|No| E[Can it give check or threaten]
  E -->|Yes| F[Insert that move first]
  E -->|No| G[Are two pieces hanging at once]
  G -->|Yes| H[Capture first with the better trade]
  G -->|No| B
```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — Mutual-capture race

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . r . P P
1 . . . . . . R K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White rook on g1 and Black rook on e2 both attack along lines; White moves.*
**FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/8/8/4r1PP/6RK w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**

White's rook is not actually attacked here, but if Black threatens ...Rxg2+ winning a pawn with check, White should preempt. **1.Rxg7!** is not available since g7 is a pawn defended by the king — instead the lesson: when *both* rooks are en prise in a real race, the side to move captures first. Here White simply consolidates; the desperado principle (capture before being captured) applies when your unit is the one under attack.

### Example 2 — Loose-piece desperado

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . n . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Black knight on e4 is far from help; if attacked it should grab a pawn first.*
**FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/4n3/8/5PPP/7K b - - 0 1
Side to move: **Black**

If White's king or a pawn were to threaten the knight, Black plays **1...Nxf2+!** — the knight, before being chased, forks nothing but wins a pawn with check, improving the trade. The doomed knight sells itself for a pawn plus tempo rather than retreating empty-handed.

## Common mistakes

- **Retreating a doomed piece for free** instead of capturing on the way out.
- **Recapturing automatically** when an in-between desperado capture or check wins more.
- **Losing the race:** in a mutual-capture situation, capturing with the wrong piece or one move too slow.
- **Ignoring checks:** a desperado check gains a tempo that can flip the material count.
- **Forgetting your own loose pieces** while hunting the opponent's — count both sides.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** filter for `intermezzo` and `hangingPiece`; do 20 reps focusing only on "what is already lost, and what can it take first."
- **Mutual-capture drills:** set up two hanging rooks/minors and force yourself to find the correct capture order — 10 positions.
- **Books:** *Reassess Your Chess Workbook* (Silman) and *Understanding Chess Tactics* (Weteschnik) cover desperado and intermezzo explicitly.
- **Blitz check-in:** in every game, when a piece is attacked, ask "is it savable? If not, what does it take first?" — turn it into a reflex over 30 games.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/2b5/8/6PP/2R1r2K w - - 0 1 — White to move. Find the correct capture.
2. **FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/4n3/8/5PPP/7K b - - 0 1 — Black to move; the knight is to be attacked next. What should it do for maximum value?
3. **FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3r2K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move, no good capture exists. State the correct verdict about a desperado here.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Rxe1!** — White's rook is attacked by the bishop and is doomed, so it captures Black's rook first, equalizing material instead of being lost for nothing. This is the mutual-capture desperado.
2. **1...Nxf2+!** — the knight will be hunted, so it grabs the f2-pawn *with check*, gaining a tempo and a pawn before it can be traded, rather than retreating for free.
3. **No desperado applies.** White's pieces are not attacked, and the lone Black rook on d1 is the loose unit. The desperado idea only fires when *your own* piece is doomed; here White simply plays normally and the rook is Black's problem, not White's.

</details>

## Related

- [Zwischenzug / Intermezzo](intermezzo.md) — [[intermezzo]]
- [Removing the defender](removing-the-defender.md) — [[removing-the-defender]]
- [Counting and exchanges](counting.md) — [[counting]]
- [Deflection](deflection.md) — [[deflection]]

1	# The Desperado
2	
3	> A doomed piece, instead of being passively captured for nothing, first grabs material or creates a threat — selling itself as dearly as possible.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
6	**Prerequisites:** [Counting and material](counting.md), [Pins](pin.md), none beyond basic tactics
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	Sometimes you have a piece that is simply lost — it is trapped, attacked twice, or part of a forced mutual exchange. The amateur reaction is to sigh and let it be taken, or to retreat it to a square where it still dies. The strong reaction is to ask: *if this piece is dying anyway, what can it take on the way down?*
11	
12	A **desperado** is that doomed piece grabbing the most valuable thing it can reach — a pawn, a piece, or even just an important square — before it is captured. Because the piece was going to be lost regardless, every scrap it collects first is pure profit. The classic setting is a **mutual-capture sequence**: two pieces are both hanging and both will come off the board. Whoever is to move can use their doomed piece to take an *extra* pawn first, then the recapture happens as expected. The result is the same piece trade plus a free pawn.
13	
14	## Why it matters
15	
16	Desperado thinking flips a losing accident into a small material gain or a saved half-point. In practical play it shows up constantly: a knight gets trapped in the opening, two minor pieces are simultaneously en prise after a tactical flurry, or a pinned piece is about to fall. Players who *don't* see desperados leak material in exactly these scattered, hard-to-calculate moments. Players who *do* squeeze a pawn (or a tempo) out of every dying piece. Over a game, that is the difference between "I lost a piece" and "I lost a piece but won two pawns and the initiative." It also sharpens your calculation: you stop evaluating captures one at a time and start asking which piece *must* be traded, then optimizing the order.
17	
18	## Diagrams
19	
20	```
21	8 . . . . r . k .
22	7 p p . . . p p p
23	6 . . . . . . . .
24	5 . . . n N . . .
25	4 . . . . . . . .
26	3 . . . . . . . .
27	2 P P . . . P P P
28	1 . . . . R . K .
29	  a b c d e f g h
30	```
31	*Both knights are attacked and one trade is unavoidable — White to move profits with a desperado.*
32	**FEN:** r5k1/pp3ppp/8/3nN3/8/8/PP3PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
33	*White to move.*
34	https://lichess.org/analysis/r5k1/pp3ppp/8/3nN3/8/8/PP3PPP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1
35	
36	The knights on d5 and e5 attack each other; whoever captures, the other recaptures with a pawn. But White's e5-knight is also *itself* attacked by Black's d5-knight, so a knight trade is coming. White plays the desperado **1.Nxf7!** — grabbing a pawn while doomed. After **1...Nxf7** (or 1...Kxf7 2.Rxe8) White has simply won a pawn compared with the dull 1.Nxd5 Rxe1+. The dying e5-knight cashed in before disappearing.
37	
38	```
39	8 . . . . . . k .
40	7 p p . . . p . p
41	6 . . . . . . . .
42	5 . . b . . . . .
43	4 . . . . . . . .
44	3 . . . . . . B .
45	2 P P . . . P P P
46	1 . . . . . . K .
47	  a b c d e f g h
48	```
49	*Two bishops stare down the a1–h8 and related diagonals; a mutual capture is forced.*
50	**FEN:** 6k1/pp3p1p/8/2b5/8/6B1/PP3PPP/6K1 b - - 0 1
51	*Black to move.*
52	https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/pp3p1p/8/2b5/8/6B1/PP3PPP/6K1_b_-_-_0_1
53	
54	Imagine the bishops are about to be exchanged. Rather than a neutral swap, the side to move first uses the doomed bishop to snatch the most valuable reachable target (here a queenside pawn with ...Bxf2+ ideas or grabbing on the long diagonal), forcing the opponent to recapture afterward. The principle: **capture order is a free variable — spend the dying piece first.**
55	
56	## Patterns and sub-types
57	
58	- **Mutual en-prise pieces:** two pieces both hanging; the one that will be traded grabs an extra pawn/piece before the swap.
59	- **Trapped-piece desperado:** a piece with no safe retreat (boxed-in bishop/knight) takes whatever it can before falling.
60	- **Pinned-piece desperado:** a pinned piece that is going to be won is used to capture along its line first.
61	- **Check-interrupted desperado:** the dying piece gives a check or creates a threat so the opponent cannot calmly recapture.
62	- **In-between (zwischenzug) flavor:** inserting the desperado capture *before* the expected recapture — closely related to [Zwischenzug](zwischenzug.md).
63	
64	## How to spot it
65	
66	```mermaid
67	flowchart TD
68	    A[A piece of mine is doomed] --> B{Can it escape with equal value}
69	    B -->|Yes| C[Save it normally]
70	    B -->|No| D{Is a mutual capture coming}
71	    D -->|Yes| E[Pick capture order so my piece grabs first]
72	    D -->|No| F{Can the dying piece take or threaten anything}
73	    F -->|Yes| G[Play the desperado grab]
74	    F -->|No| H[Accept the loss minimize damage]
75	    E --> G
76	```
77	
78	## Worked examples
79	
80	```
81	8 . . . q . . k .
82	7 . . . . . p p p
83	6 . . . . . . . .
84	5 . . . Q . . . .
85	4 . . . . . . . .
86	3 . . . . . . . .
87	2 . . . . . P P P
88	1 . . . . . . K .
89	  a b c d e f g h
90	```
91	*White to move — the queens face off on the d-file and a trade is forced.*
92	**FEN:** 3q2k1/5ppp/8/3Q4/8/8/5PPP/6K1 w - - 0 1
93	**Solution:** The queens will be exchanged. Instead of 1.Qxd8+ Kxd8 (a sterile trade), White desperadoes with **1.Qxd8+** only if it wins — here it is a plain swap, so the lesson is the *order*: if either queen could first capture a loose pawn or deliver a winning check, take that first. With nothing extra to grab, the position is equal — a reminder that desperado pays only when the dying piece can collect.
94	
95	```
96	8 . . . . . . k .
97	7 p p . . . p p p
98	6 . . . . . . . .
99	5 . . n N . . . .
100	4 . . . . . . . .
101	3 . . . . . . . .
102	2 P P . . . P P P
103	1 . . . . . . K .
104	  a b c d e f g h
105	```
106	*Black to move — knights on c5 and d5 attack each other.*
107	**FEN:** 6k1/pp3ppp/8/2nN4/8/8/PP3PPP/6K1 b - - 0 1
108	**Solution:** A knight trade is forced. Black's c5-knight will be lost to the recapture, so it desperadoes: **1...Nxb2** snatches the a-file pawn (the knight on b2 is then itself trapped but Black has already banked the pawn, and 2...Nxd5 is unavailable since it was the d5-knight doing the work). The cleaner motif: **1...Nxd5** trades evenly, but if the c5-knight can grab a loose pawn with check or fork first, do that before allowing the swap. Always test the dying knight's captures.
109	
110	## Common mistakes
111	
112	- Recapturing "automatically" without checking whether your doomed piece could have grabbed something first.
113	- Forgetting that **capture order is yours to choose** in a mutual exchange.
114	- Sending the dying piece to a passive square instead of an aggressive capture/check.
115	- Miscounting: make sure the desperado grab doesn't leave a *bigger* piece hanging elsewhere.
116	- Overlooking that the opponent may have an in-between check that refutes your desperado.
117	
118	## Train it
119	
120	- **Lichess puzzle themes:** train the `desperado` theme directly at lichess.org/training/desperado; also drill `zwischenzug` and `hangingPiece`. Do **20 puzzles per session, 3 sessions a week**.
121	- **Drill:** set up two mutually-attacked pieces on a board and find the most profitable capture order. **10 reps**, alternating colors.
122	- **Books:** *Chess Tactics from Scratch* (Weteschnik) — chapter on intermediate moves; *Understanding Chess Tactics* (Weteschnik); *1001 Winning Chess Sacrifices and Combinations* (Reinfeld) for raw pattern volume.
123	- **Habit:** every time a piece of yours is attacked twice, ask "what can it take first?" before moving anything.
124	
125	## Exercises
126	
127	1. **FEN:** r5k1/pp3ppp/8/3nN3/8/8/PP3PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1 — *White to move.* Win a pawn using a desperado.
128	2. **FEN:** 6k1/pp3ppp/8/2nN4/8/8/PP3PPP/6K1 b - - 0 1 — *Black to move.* Find the best capture order for the doomed knight.
129	3. **FEN:** 3q2k1/5ppp/8/3Q4/8/8/5PPP/6K1 w - - 0 1 — *White to move.* Is there a desperado, or only a neutral trade? Explain.
130	
131	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
132	
133	1. **1.Nxf7!** The e5-knight is lost to ...Nxe5 anyway, so it grabs the f7-pawn first. After 1...Nxf7 (1...Kxf7 2.Rxe8) White is a clean pawn up versus the bland 1.Nxd5 Rxe1+.

The cleanest, most famous desperado is the **mutual queen capture** — when two queens are en prise simultaneously and one can grab a pawn (or capture with check) before the forced recapture. input.command:

=== CLEANEST DESPERADO: mutual QUEEN capture, the famous motif ===
Two queens attack each other AND are both defended only by pawns.
The side to move uses its doomed queen to grab a pawn (or capture
something extra) WITH the queen before queens are traded.

Classic minimal: White Qd5, Black Qd8 on open d-file, both back up.
Qd5 attacks d8 (file) - yes if nothing between. Qd8 attacks d5 - yes.
Defenders: White Qd5 defended by? nothing -> if Black plays Qxd5 wins queen!
So we need them mutually defended so it's a TRADE, then desperado grabs extra.

THE textbook desperado (from Weteschnik / standard texts):
White Q and Black Q en prise to each other; White's queen, before being
captured, snaps off a loose pawn WITH CHECK, then recaptures the queen.

Minimal verified construction:
White: Kg1, Qe4, P f2 g2 h2, a2 b2
Black: Kg8, Qe7, P f7 g7 h7, a7 b7
Qe4 and Qe7 on e-file, mutually attacking (open between e5,e6). 
Are they defended? Qe4 defended by? nothing. Qe7 defended by? nothing.
If White Qxe7 then nothing recaptures (no black piece guards e7) -> White just wins the queen. Not a trade. Not desperado. 

Need the queens DEFENDED so capture = trade. 
Put black Qe7 defended by Kf8? Move Black king to f8. And White Qe4 
defended by... need a white piece. This grows.

Hmm. A pure mutual-capture desperado needs each captured square defended
by a same-or-pawn unit. Easiest with PAWNS guarding.

CONSTRUCT mutual queen trade where desperado wins a pawn-with-check:
White Qd4, defended by white pawn c3. Black Qd6, defended by black pawn c7.
Qd4 and Qd6 mutually attack on d-file (d5 empty). 
1.Qxd6 cxd6 = even queen trade.  Desperado: can white queen grab something
with check BEFORE? If Black has a loose pawn on, say, a7 and white queen
route... queen can't both stay on d-file capturing and grab a7. 
The desperado idea for queens: Qd4 takes a pawn that ALSO keeps attacking
the black queen OR gives check. 

Simplest clean win-a-pawn desperado that I can FULLY verify:
=== Use a ROOK mutual-capture on a rank, the truly minimal classic ===
White Rc7, Black Rc8? Rc7 attacks c8 (file). Rc8 attacks c7. Mutual.
Rc7 (white) attacks black pawns on 7th rank: a7,b7 etc (along 7th rank).
Rc7 defended? Rc8 defended by king?

input.command:
cat << 'EOF'
=== THE CLASSIC BISHOP DESPERADO (Reinfeld / standard) ===
This is the textbook example. Setup:
White bishop and Black bishop are BOTH attacked (each by a pawn), and a 
bishop trade is in the air. The side to move's bishop, being doomed, 
captures an EXTRA pawn first; then bishops are exchanged. Net: +1 pawn.

VERIFIED minimal construction:
White: Kg1, Bd5, pawns a2 b2 f2 g2 h2
Black: Kg8, Be6, pawns a7 b7 f7 g7 h7  -- but Bd5 & Be6 adjacent diagonal, mutual attack: Bd5 attacks e6 YES; Be6 attacks d5 YES. Good. But neither is doomed (no pawn attacks them). Just a normal trade.

For DESPERADO we need each bishop ALREADY attacked by a pawn so the trade
is forced, then one grabs extra.

Better — the genuine famous one is two bishops that have just captured into
a position where each can capture a pawn. Let me build the cleanest:

White Bb5, Black Bb4 (both on b-file? bishops can't be on same file attacking).
Bishops attack diagonally. Two opposite bishops attacking each other are on
the same diagonal, adjacent or with empty squares between.

Bc4 (white) and Bf7 (black)? Not same diagonal. 
Bc4 white on a2-g8 diagonal. Be6 black on that diagonal -> Bc4 sees d5,e6. mutual with Be6.

THE point of "sells dearly" - clearest with a TRAPPED piece, not mutual.
Let me do BOTH a trapped-piece and a mutual cleanly:

=== POSITION A (MAIN): Trapped bishop desperado - fully minimal & checkable ===
A white bishop is trapped (no safe square) and will be lost. Before dying,
it captures a pawn.
White: Kg1, Bh7 (trapped on h7), pawns f2 g2 h2 a2 b2
Black: Kg8, pawns a7 b7 g6 (g6 traps the bishop? bishop h7 can go g8(occupied by king? no king g8) ...). 
Bishop on h7: diagonal squares g8 (black king there - can't), g6 (if black pawn g6, Bxg6). 
So Bh7 trapped by pawn on g6 and king on g8. It can only move to g6 capturing the pawn.
Desperado: Bxg6! grabs a pawn before ...fxg6 or ...Kxbishop? After Bxg6, what recaptures? 
black pawn f7xg6 -> yes fxg6. So White trades B for one pawn. That's a LOSS (B for P). Not good.

Trapped-piece desperado only "profits" relative to losing it for NOTHING.
i.e., the bishop was going to be lost for free; grabbing a pawn first softens it.
THAT is the correct teaching: doomed for free -> instead takes a pawn. Net you lose B for P instead of B for nothing.

That IS a legitimate desperado but let me make sure the bishop truly had NO
escape and was lost for free otherwise. Good enough as Example.

=== POSITION B (MAIN, mutual-capture, the strongest illustration) ===
Two knights mutually attacking, BOTH defended by pawns => knight trade forced.
The doomed knight grabs an extra pawn with the trade still even.
This needs: WNe5 <-> BNc6 (mutual). WNe5 defended by Wpawn? put pawn d4 (d4 defends e5). 
BNc6 defended by Bpawn b7 (b7 defends c6) or d7 (d7 defends c6).
WNe5 can capture black pawns at: c6(the knight),d7,f7,g6,c4(own side),etc. d7 & f7 are black pawns.
1.Nxd7! white knight takes pawn d7. Is Nxd7 still leaving an even trade? After 1.Nxd7,
black recaptures? d7 knight attacked by? black king or pawn? If Black Kg8, and pawn c8? 
Hmm need black to recapture the knight to keep it a "trade". 
After 1.Nxd7 the white knight sits on d7; black Nc6 no longer attacked it... 

This is getting circular. The TRUE desperado mutual-capture works like:
Position where if White plays the natural recapture 1.Nxc6, Black answers 1...bxc6 (even).
But White has 1.Nxf7! first (grabbing a pawn) and after 1...Nxe5 (black grabs the white... wait white knight left e5).

I think the cleanest is the QUEEN race below. Let me lock the queen one.
EOF

content.text: Now I understand the precise mechanics. The classic mutual-capture desperado works like this: **White's bishop and Black's bishop are mutually attacked (a trade is forced). Instead of the natural recapture, the doomed piece grabs an extra pawn first; the opponent's piece then also desperadoes, and material stays even but you netted a pawn.** Let me build the canonical version exactly.

input.command:
cat << 'EOF'
=== THE CANONICAL MUTUAL-CAPTURE DESPERADO (verified step by step) ===

Setup so that BOTH bishops are doomed (mutually attacked AND each can grab a pawn).
The trick: each bishop, instead of capturing the other, first grabs the enemy
pawn it also attacks; net both bishops trade off but BOTH grab a pawn = wash,
EXCEPT the side to move grabs first and can choose the bigger pawn / get tempo.

Cleanest real version where moving FIRST wins a pawn:
Two rooks on the same file, mutually attacking, each ALSO able to capture a pawn
on the 7th/2nd rank. Whoever moves first plays Rxpawn (desperado) and the opponent
can only recapture the rook OR desperado their own rook taking ONE pawn — but the
first mover already grabbed a pawn AND threatens more.

THE textbook minimal (this is the standard one taught):
White: Kg1, Rc2(on c-file), pawns a2 f2 g2 h2  ... 
Black: Kg8, Rc7(on c-file), pawns a7 f7 g7 h7
Rc2 and Rc7 mutually attack along open c-file. A rook trade looms.
White to move: 1.Rxc7 just trades rooks (even).
BUT desperado: 1.Rxa7! — wait does Rc2 reach a7? No, Rc2 is on c-file/2nd rank.
Rc2 along 2nd rank reaches a2(own),b2,d2.. and along c-file c1..c7. Can't reach a7.

For the rook desperado the rooks must be on the 7th rank (white) / 2nd (black)
where they each rake a row of enemy pawns. THE classic:

White Rb7 (7th rank), Black Rb2 (2nd rank). They are on the SAME b-file -> 
mutually attack along b-file (if open). 
White Rb7 attacks black pawns on 7th rank: a7, c7, etc.
Black Rb2 attacks white pawns on 2nd rank: a2, c2, etc.
Both rooks doomed (can trade on b-file). It's a DESPERADO RACE:
side to move grabs a pawn on the 7th, opponent grabs one on the 2nd, etc.
First mover stays a pawn ahead because they grab first AND opponent must
eventually let rooks trade.

VERIFIED POSITION:
White: Kg1, Rb7, pawns a2 f2 g2 h2
Black: Kg8, Rb2, pawns a7 f7 g7 h7

Setup so that BOTH bishops are doomed (mutually attacked AND each can grab a pawn).
The trick: each bishop, instead of capturing the other, first grabs the enemy
pawn it also attacks; net both bishops trade off but BOTH grab a pawn = wash,

Cleanest real version where moving FIRST wins a pawn:
Two rooks on the same file, mutually attacking, each ALSO able to capture a pawn
on the 7th/2nd rank. Whoever moves first plays Rxpawn (desperado) and the opponent
can only recapture the rook OR desperado their own rook taking ONE pawn — but the
first mover already grabbed a pawn AND threatens more.

side to move grabs a pawn on the 7th, opponent grabs one on the 2nd, etc.

eventually let rooks trade.

=== LOCKING FINAL POSITIONS — full verification ===

The mutual-capture desperado nets a pawn when BOTH pieces grab, but the FIRST
mover takes the more valuable item, OR grabs with check to gain a tempo, OR
(cleanest) the trade is forced and only the side to move can grab a pawn at all.

==== DIAGRAM 1 / EXAMPLE 1 : mutual bishop capture, side-to-move grabs a pawn ====
The truly clean version: two bishops mutually attack on a diagonal. The recapture
is forced (bishop must be taken or trade). The side to move plays Bx(pawn) where
that SAME bishop, after grabbing the pawn, STILL attacks the enemy bishop OR is
the only one who can grab. 

Cleanest verified: 
White Bb3, Black Bb6? Not same diagonal. 
White Bd5 & Black Bb7 on a8-h1 diagonal: Bd5 sees c6,b7(black B),e4,f3... ; Bb7 sees c6,d5(white B). 
MUTUAL d5<->b7 (c6 empty). 
Bd5 (white) also attacks: along a2-g8 diag: e6,f7(black pawn!), and c4,b3,a2; along a8-h1: c6,b7,e4,f3,g2,h1.
So Bd5 attacks f7 pawn. 
Bb7 (black) also attacks: along a8-h1: c6,d5,a8; along a6-c8...: a8,c8; along b7 other diag (a6,c8) and (a8). 
Bb7 attacks white pawn? put white pawn g2? Bb7 sees c6,d5,e4,f3,g2(white pawn!),h1. Yes Bb7 attacks g2.
So BOTH bishops attack each other AND each attacks one enemy pawn (Bd5->f7, Bb7->g2).
This is a symmetric desperado race -> ends even. NOT a clean "win a pawn".

CONCLUSION: a symmetric mutual-capture is EVEN. The lesson there is "don't lose
the race / capture in the right order", which IS valid desperado content but
isn't a "win material" tactic. I'll present mutual-capture honestly as a TEMPO/
order lesson, and use the TRAPPED-PIECE desperado as the "win material" example.

==== DIAGRAM 1 (MAIN): TRAPPED BISHOP grabs a pawn before dying ====
White bishop trapped deep in Black's camp, lost next move; it grabs a pawn first.
White: Kg1, Bh7 ; Black: Kg8 ... but Kg8 next to Bh7, and Bh7 attacks g8? bishop h7
attacks g8 (diagonal) -> that would be check on the black king, illegal setup if
Black to move not in check... Let me place black king elsewhere.

Trapped bishop on a8 (corner) — classic "bishop trapped on a8/h8".
White Ba8, trapped by black pawn b7. Ba8 can only go to b7 (capturing) -> after Bxb7, 
black king or pawn recaptures. If only loss available, grabbing b7 = B for P (better than B for nothing).
But is Ba8 "lost for nothing" otherwise? If Black plays ...Kb8 (no), the bishop just
sits; Black wins it by ...Bb7-ish. The standard "trapped on a8" the bishop is lost.
Grabbing b7 first is the desperado.

Hmm, even simpler and 100% clean to verify, use a KNIGHT fork-desperado:

==== EXAMPLE 1 (clean, wins material): doomed knight captures a rook ====
Black Nb3 attacked by white pawn a2 (a2xb3). All knight retreats are covered.
Knight b3 destinations: a1,c1,d2,d4,c5,a5.
We want Black to play ...Nxd2 or ...Nxc1 grabbing a WHITE ROOK as the desperado.
Put White Rc1. b3->c1 is a knight move (yes). So ...Nxc1 grabs the rook.
Is the knight otherwise just lost-for-nothing? If Black DIDN'T desperado and moved
Na5 (safe square a5), the knight escapes. To force desperado, cover the safe squares.
But honestly the desperado lesson is: knight is ATTACKED; instead of retreating to
safety it CAPTURES the more valuable rook. That's a desperado/zwischenzug capture and
is sound regardless. After ...Nxc1, White's a2 pawn can't recapture (c1 not reachable
by a2). White recaptures with Rxc1 only if another rook — none. So Black simply wins
the exchange (or a rook). Clean and SOUND. Use it.

black king g8, white king g1.

By a pawn? no. So Black wins a whole rook with the doomed knight. SOUND. 

Wait — is the knight actually "doomed"? It's only attacked by the a2 pawn; Black could 
also just play ...Nd4 or ...Nc5 to safety. So it's not strictly trapped. The desperado
framing: the knight is under attack and rather than flee it grabs the rook — net Black
is up a full rook either way. Present honestly: "the attacked knight, instead of 
retreating for nothing, captures the rook." Good, and unambiguously winning.
